title: CloudFront Cache Keys, Origin Requests, TTLs, and Invalidation · Language Lens: Origin Request Policy
summary: A cache key includes every viewer header, authorization is omitted, the origin receives different data than the cache varies on, and releases rely on global invalidation as routine deployment. Predict the representation, storage, trust, cost, and recovery evidence before inspecting a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the origin request policy increment in cache-key, forwarding, and freshness contract. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal deb…
objectives: Use origin request policy for additional required viewer data without unnecessary cache fragmentation or trust escalation.
Include only representation-changing path, query, header, cookie, encoding, and authorization dimensions in the cache key.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Predict Origin Request Policy · Theory Origin Request Policy
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go file-delivery team handles case 4787 during a private media release. Build deterministic Go evidence to align cache identity, origin forwarding, TTL, versioned names, and urgent invalidation; retain stable tenant and file identity without exposing it in metric labels; then cancel halfway through and prove every body, part, and goroutine reaches a terminal state. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Origin Request Policy contract for the supplied data.
